sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 45-year-old male who presents with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his son.
He reports landing awkwardly and twisting his ankle, resulting in swelling and tenderness on the outside of the ankle.
He mentions that he has been having trouble walking and that the ankle feels unstable.
He has been working out regularly and does not want to disrupt his routine.
His son, who is 10 years old, is a big fan of Stephen Curry and enjoys playing basketball.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's ankle is swollen, with exquisite tenderness on the lateral and medial deltoid regions.
The skin is intact, and capillary refill is brisk.
There is no malalignment.
Test Results:
X-ray results: A displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a displaced lateral malleolar fracture that requires surgical intervention.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam to reduce swelling, and instructed the patient to ice the ankle and elevate it regularly.
I recommended that he use crutches to avoid putting weight on the ankle.
I advised him to continue coaching his son's basketball team, as he is able to do so without putting excessive stress on the ankle.
I estimated that he will be out for approximately three months.
Follow-up: I will follow up with the patient to assess his progress and adjust his treatment plan as necessary.
